title: Introducing a buck to the herd
subtitle: Managing mating season and the buck's impact on the dairy herd.
excerpt: A buck running with a dairy herd taints the milk with his scent gland secretions within days. The practical approach is to pen him separately, use sight-only contact to bring does into oestrus, then allow timed mating. The buck effect reliably synchronises cycles across a herd within three weeks.
sourceNotes: British Goat Society kidding and mating guidance, britishgoatsociety.com. DEFRA Code of Recommendations for the Welfare of Goats. SAC Consulting small ruminant breeding notes.

glossaryTerms.definition: The phenomenon where the sudden presence of a male goat stimulates ovulation in does that had been seasonally anoestrous. A buck introduced to a female-only herd in late summer typically induces oestrus within 17 to 21 days.
The fertile period of the doe's cycle when she will accept mating. Goats cycle approximately every 21 days during the breeding season.
A vasectomised male used to stimulate the buck effect without risking unwanted matings. Also called a cut buck.

content.text: is one of the most reliable tools in small-scale goat management. A buck that has been separated from does for at least a month before introduction triggers a wave of synchronised
in the herd within three weeks. This compresses the kidding window and allows planned management of the kidding period.

An intact buck in peak breeding condition produces a strong odour from secretion glands behind the horns. If he runs with milking does, the scent contaminates the milk within a few days, making it unpleasant or unusable. Even does that sleep near a buck without contact will show taint in their milk.

The solution is to keep the buck in a separate pen with a fence that allows nose contact but not free mixing. Adjacent paddocks with a shared wire fence are adequate; the buck's presence and smell stimulate the does without direct access.

If you want kids born in February or March for spring milk, introduce the buck or use fence contact from mid-September. Does in the UK goat breeds begin cycling naturally from August onwards, and the buck effect accelerates the first oestrus.

Watch for signs of oestrus in the does: restlessness, tail wagging, frequent urination, mounting other does, standing to be mounted, swollen and moist vulva. A doe in full oestrus will stand for the buck and should be put with him for twenty-four hours.

can run with the herd freely without contaminating milk or causing unwanted pregnancies. He stimulates oestrus just as an intact buck does. When a doe is observed in standing oestrus she is moved to the intact buck for a timed mating. This method gives you both the buck effect and control over which sire is used.

After the initial wave of oestrus (day seventeen to twenty-one after buck introduction), expect a second wave twenty-one days later. Does that were not mated in the first wave or did not conceive will come back into oestrus. Keep a mating record: date of service, which buck, any observed signs of pregnancy at three weeks (no return to oestrus).

A doe carrying multiples is more likely in autumn if she has been well flushed (gaining body condition) in the weeks before mating. Body condition score 2.5 to 3.5 at mating, rising slightly, is the target. Does in poor condition or already fat are less likely to conceive and more likely to have kidding difficulties.
